bio.education: Profile High School (Bethlehem, N.H.) ‘ 04 University of New Hampshire ‘08, Geography, International Affairs
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Walker was born with lumbar sacral agenesis, a condition that resulted in his spine missing after the first vertebra; he had both legs amputated at the knee at age four
  • He grew up skiing in adaptive programs at Waterville Valley and Loon Mountain, eventually joining the New England Disabled Ski Team. At just 19, he was the world cup overall runner-up for giant slalom. A year later, he earned his first trip to the Paralympic Winter Games and captured the giant slalom overall world cup title. Since then, Walker has been a constant force on the international stage
  • Son of James and Carol Walker
  • Has one sister, Ashley
  • Minored in German and political science
  • Hobbies include fishing, kayaking and exploring
  • Gained acclaim in Colorado Springs, Colorado, when he hiked the Manitou Incline, which boasts more than 2,000 feet in elevation gain in less than one mile, on his hands.
Paralympic Experience
  • 4-time Paralympian; 2-time Paralympic medalist (2 silver)
    • Paralympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, (Super Combined - Sitting - Men), silver (Giant Slalom - Sitting - Men, Slalom - Sitting - Men), 12th (Super-G - Sitting - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Sochi 2014, (Downhill - Sitting - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Vancouver 2010, (Downhill - Sitting - Men), 10th (Slalom - Sitting - Men), 16th (Giant Slalom - Sitting - Men), 22th (Super-G - Sitting - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Turin 2006, 6th (Giant Slalom - Sitting - Men), 11th (Super-G - Sitting - Men), 14th (Slalom - Sitting - Men), 15th (Downhill - Sitting - Men)
World Championship Experience

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Speciality: Sled Hockey, Paracanoe (KL3)
  • Son of John and Barbara Wallace
  • Has three siblings – Colleen, Matt and Mike
  • Favorite food is buffalo chicken pizza
  • His idol is Martin Brodeur
  • Hobbies include golf, video games, cooking and playing guitar
Biographical Information

Jack Wallace was a five-sport athlete who competed in hockey, football, baseball, lacrosse and wrestling while growing up in New Jersey. At the age of 10, Wallace lost part of his right leg in a boating accident while on vacation. The following year, while he attended Camp No Limits, a camp for children and families dealing with limb loss, he discovered sled hockey and made his U.S. National Sled Hockey Team debut in 2016. 

Wallace is a two-time Paralympic gold medalist with the U.S. sled hockey team, including being named best defenseman at the Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022. The dual-sport athlete has also been a member of the U.S. Paracanoe national team since 2022. In 2024, he participated in the same week in the World Para Ice Hockey Championships in Canada and the ParaCanoe Championships in Hungary, where he attempted to qualify for the Paralympic Games Paris 2024 in Paracanoe, falling just a few hundredths of a second short of achieving this goal. 

When he is not competing in either sport, Wallace enjoys golf, cooking, playing guitar and spending time with his dog, Maggie, a rescue brindle pit bull mix.

Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian; 3-time Paralympic medalist (3 gold)
    • Paralympic Games Milan 2026, gold (Team - Mixed)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2022, gold (Team - Mixed)
    • Paralympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, gold (Team - Mixed)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – gold (Team - Men)
  • Years of participation: Team - Men 2017, 2019, 2021, 2023, 2024, 2025
  • Medals: 6 (4 gold, 2 silver)
    • Gold – 2025 (Team - Men); 2023 (Team - Men); 2021 (Team - Men); 2019 (Team - Men)
    • Silver – 2024 (Team - Men); 2017 (Team - Men)

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Son of Jeff and Sabina Wallace. Has one older sister, Brittany. Married to Lea Babcock, and owns a Hungarian Vizsla named Luna
  • Grew up in an athletic family as the son of a collegiate tennis coach and former all-conference distance runner at the University of Georgia
  • During his junior year in 2010, he started feeling pain in his right shin then ultimately learned it was the effect of compartment syndrome
  • Complications in surgery caused him to make the decision to amputate his leg below the knee
  • Came across the world record list for Paralympic track and field, subsequently showing his parents and telling them that his name would be there
  • Within 12 weeks of his amputation, he had his first running blade and just 15 months after his amputation, he won his first major international gold and set a world record at the 2011 Parapan American Games in Guadalajara, Mexico
  • Started a Leg in Faith Foundation in 2012 that gives grants to amputees 18-year-old and over who are dedicated to becoming future U.S. Paralympians
  • Works part-time as a territory representative for a prosthetic company called Shamrock Prosthetics, where he coaches patients through their amputation and recovery
  • Hobbies include music, playing guitar, wakeboarding, surfing, writing and reading
Paralympic Experience
  • 4-time Paralympian; 2-time Paralympic medalist (2 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Paris 2024, bronze (Long Jump T64 - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, bronze (200m T64 - Men), 6th (100m T64 - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, (4 x 100m Relay T42-47 - Men), 5th (100m T44 - Men)
    • Paralympic Games London 2012, (4 x 100m Relay T42-46 - Men), 6th (400m T44 - Men)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2023 – bronze (Long Jump T64 - Men)
  • Years of participation: Long Jump T64 - Men 2023; 100m T44 - Men 2013, 2017; 200m T44 - Men 2013, 2017; 4 x 100m Relay T42-47 - Men 2017; 4 x 100m Relay T42-46 - Men 2013
  • Medals: 5 (3 gold, 2 bronze)
    • Gold – 2017 (200m T44 - Men); 2013 (200m T44 - Men, 4 x 100m Relay T42-46 - Men)
    • Bronze – 2023 (Long Jump T64 - Men); 2017 (100m T44 - Men)

Quick Facts
  • Parents are Lynn and Kim Wallace
  • Dream job is to work at ESPN
  • Favorite movie is Fast and the Furious
  • Three words to describe her are: fearless, hard working and caring
  • On what it means to play for USA Softball: It is a tremendous honor that only a few get to experience. It has been a life long dream, especially since they have put softball back into the Olympics (in 2020).
  • On what she enjoys most about playing softball: Being able to compete against the best with the people who I have made strong friendships with
Career Highlights
  • U-19 World Cup, Irvine, 2019 (Team - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • 2019 WBSC U-19 Women's Softball World Cup Gold Medalist; Hit .448 (13-for-29) with 1 HR, 6 RBI and 19 runs scored

Quick Facts
Daughter of John Walsh • Siblings include; Jennifer Walsh, Caitlin Richardson and Glen John • Dog: Camillionairre Caboom (Camilla) • Holds a degree in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, and a master's degree in Executive Leadership • Lost her vision at the age of 14 as a result of a pediatric brain tumor and complications from surgery • Founded Blind Ambition Speaking and helps others overcome their visual impairments • Worked as a software engineer at Microsoft for six years • Published her autobiography, "Blind Ambition: How to Envision Your Limitless Potential and Achieve the Success You Want," in 2014

Quick Facts
  • Walsh is a retired New York state trooper and mother to three children – two sons, Griffin and Sullivan, and one daughter, Julia
  • Married to Greg Walsh
  • As an avid triathlete, Walsh’s multiple sclerosis began to impact her ability to bike and run
  • However, Walsh found a way to keep her competitive spirit alive through competing in the trike class in Paralympic cycling events
  • In her first world championships, Walsh came away with silver in the road race and bronze in the time trial

bio.education: Savannah College of Art and Design ‘17
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • At the age of two, Walsh began skiing and by the age of five was ski racing
  • As an avid athlete, he was also active in the arts
  • He began acting at the age of six and continued to be involved with acting, music and dance alongside numerous sports including Nordic skiing, hockey and triathlon
  • He was awarded a Junior National Triathlon Championship title at the age of 10
  • He was diagnosed with Ewing's sarcoma in May 2009 and had his final radiation treatment in June 2010
  • Recovering from cancer treatments, he attended Green Mountain Valley School and returned to ski racing
  • After completing his education at GMVS, he enrolled into the Savannah College of Art and Design to study performing arts
  • Thomas returned to ski racing after one year of college and the following year was invited to be on the U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Team
  • Within his first year, he had won his first Slalom and Giant Slalom World Cup races
  • Son of Kathleen Walsh
  • Hobbies include acting, writing, creating, traveling, mountain biking, film, vintage thrift shopping and being outdoors
  • He names Mikaela Shiffrin and Steven Nyman as his idols
  • As a cancer survivor, he races for all those who cannot
  • His credits his mother as the most influential person in his life and career.

World Championships years participation

Years of Participation: 2017, 2019, 2021, 2023.
Most recent: 2023 - 12th (downhill). Medals: 2 (2 bronzes).

  • Two-time Mini Chowder Cup champion, MVP (2013, 2014)

Quick Facts
  • Walsh Jennings is the daughter of Tim and Margie Walsh
  • Walsh Jennings has an older brother, Marte, two younger sisters, Kelli and KC
  • Married to beach volleyball player Casey Jennings
    • The pair has three children, sons Joseph (2009) and Sundance (2010) and daughter Scout (2013)
  • Walsh Jennings competed while pregnant with Joseph following the Olympic Games Beijing 2008
    • She was pregnant with Scout during the Olympic Games London 2012
  • Walsh Jennings was inducted into the Stanford Athletics Hall of Fame in 2011 and into the Pac-12 Hall of Honor in 2018
  • Started her company p1440 in 2018
  • Played volleyball and basketball in high school, earning a state championship title with both teams
  • Inducted into the USOPC Hall of Fame, Class of 2025
Biographical Information

Kerri Walsh Jennings is one of the most decorated athletes in beach volleyball history. A three-time Olympic gold medalist (2004, 2008, 2012) and bronze medalist (2016), she holds the record for the most career victories in both international and domestic beach volleyball, with 135 wins. A three-time world champion (2003, 2005, 2007), Walsh Jennings also set records for the most consecutive match wins (112) and tournament wins (19).

Alongside longtime partner Misty May-Treanor, she achieved an unprecedented 21 consecutive Olympic match victories across three Olympic Games. Walsh Jennings’ resilience, dominance and dedication have left an enduring legacy in the sport of beach volleyball and beyond. Despite undergoing five shoulder surgeries, she returned to win the Rio Grand Slam in 2016. In one of her most memorable feats, she competed at the Olympic Games London 2012 while five weeks pregnant.
